affiliation_corrected: New York City resident and independent (unaffiliated) voter; open-primaries advocate associated with Open Primaries NYC
notes: The transcribed spelling "Jeffrey Aron" is correct. He is a documented, recurring New York City open-primaries advocate who testifies as an independent voter, matching the "Resident (independent voter)" role. The public record on citymeetings.nyc spells his name "Jeffrey Aron" (an auto-transcript there also renders it "Jeff Aaron," but "Jeffrey Aron" is the canonical form used in the meeting index and coverage). He is identified as an Open Primaries NYC supporter and has appeared on MSNBC's Morning Joe (ms.now/msnbc coverage). The "650,000 people of color excluded from voting" line is a standard Open Primaries NYC framing about independent voters — disproportionately people of color — locked out of New York's closed primaries, consistent with his known advocacy. The specific 650,000-people-of-color quote was not found verbatim in the Feb 2025 Charter Revision transcript (a different hearing than the 2026 COGE hearing), but the speaker, role and cause are an unambiguous match. Additional coverage: https://www.ms.now/news/new-york-primaries-independents-disenfranchise-gerrymandering and https://ivn.us/ny-independents-watch-dc-open-its-primaries-and-ask-why-not-us/

affiliation_corrected: Founder and CEO of Building Purpose Incorporated, Staten Island (justice-impacted advocacy nonprofit); also co-creator of RedemptionVentures.org
notes: LinkedIn profile confirms an exact match: "Edwin Hernandez - Founder | C.E.O. - Building Purpose," located in Staten Island. The transcribed spelling is correct (common name, standard spelling). Affiliation from transcript is accurate: he is founder/CEO of the nonprofit Building Purpose Inc. His profile describes 26 years mentoring justice-impacted nonprofit founders and co-founding RedemptionVentures.org (with Victor D. Lombard and Ron Bauer) to support formerly incarcerated founders. This context strongly corroborates the testimony quote about "1.2 million constituents locked out of elections... unable to cast a vote... due to discriminatory practices," which references felony-disenfranchisement/voting-rights advocacy consistent with his justice-reform work. Confidence high on both name and affiliation.

affiliation_corrected: Dr. Jessie Fields, board member and national spokesperson for Open Primaries (Harlem physician; also serves on the board of Independent Voting)
notes: Name is already correctly spelled. Dr. Jessie Fields is a well-known public figure in the open-primaries movement: a Harlem-based physician and board member/national spokesperson for Open Primaries, also on the board of Independent Voting. She has a long, well-documented record of testifying as an independent-voter advocate for opening NYC primaries to unaffiliated voters, including prior NYC Charter Revision Commission public-input sessions (citymeetings.nyc records her testimony from Open Primaries on May 19, 2025 and July 7, 2025) and amNewYork coverage of open-primary advocates pressing the Mamdani panel. The transcribed testimony line about "segregating unaffiliated voters ... is inherently unequal" is fully consistent with her documented positions (she calls closed primaries "political discrimination" and cites NYC's ~1.1 million independent voters, majority people of color). Affiliation "Independent-voter advocate (open primaries)" is accurate; refined to name the organization (Open Primaries). The exact verbatim quote was not independently located online, but identity is confirmed by the distinctive affiliation, role and consistent public record.

The affiliation given in the transcript (Families for Safe Streets member, advocacy org) is accurate. High confidence: exact-name directory match plus corroborating affiliation and PSC delegate role.

affiliation_corrected: New York City public-school paraprofessional (22+ years); elected UFT chapter leader (school-level)
notes: The name is not a transcription guess — in the hearing transcript the witness self-introduces verbatim: "My name is Katrina Long and I'm here to talk about wasted money and wasted time. I have served as a New York City paraprofessional for over 22 years... I am also an elected chapter [leader]." (local file transcripts/manhattan_r2.txt, lines 254/265/272). Because the name comes from her own spoken introduction, spelling is likely accurate, but I could not locate an independent public record confirming a "Katrina Long." Searches of UFT pages, union rosters, news, and general web returned no match. The UFT paraprofessional chapter-representatives page lists no one with the surname Long (that page covers only citywide officers/coordinators, not the thousands of building-level chapter leaders, so absence there is not disqualifying). Near-but-different matches found: Katrina Thomas (chapter leader, PS 289 Brooklyn) and Katrina Foye — neither is this person. Verdict: plausible, self-identified NYC paraprofessional and elected school-level UFT chapter leader who testified in support of the paraprofessional "RESPECT check" bill, but unconfirmed by any external public record, so name_verified=false at medium confidence. Kept the transcribed spelling. Affiliation refined to note "school-level" chapter leader.

affiliation_corrected: Centurum, an AI govtech efficiency startup (early-stage/pre-launch; not verifiable in public records); University of Oxford student. Product described as an AI tool built with Harvard and Stanford collaborators that reviews contracts/documents and judges compliance.
verify_source: https://www.beta.nyc/2026/06/11/coge-testimony/ (COGE hearing context); local transcript transcripts/manhattan_r2.txt lines 385-393 (self-identification). No confirming public record found.
notes: Transcript (Manhattan Round 2) corroborates the details: the chair announced the speaker as "George Lee" in a two-name roll call ("FISA Khaled and George Lee"), and he self-identified as "a student from Oxford University" representing "Centurum, an AI govtech efficiency startup," describing a document/contract-compliance tool built "in Oxford with a team from Harvard and Stanford." The quoted line matches verbatim ("there is a reason that that paperwork exists. That paperwork is also a huge barrier..."). Because the name came through in a clear announced roll call rather than mid-sentence, "George Lee" is a plausible spelling — hence medium, not low, confidence. However, I could NOT confirm his identity via any public record: web searches for "Centurum" as an AI govtech/document-compliance startup return nothing matching (the only "Centurum" is an unrelated Virginia defense contractor; closest name-alikes are Centauri AI, Centurium, Centuri — none a fit). The company appears to be a very early-stage or unlaunched student venture with no findable footprint, and "George Lee" is too common to disambiguate without the org anchor. Not fabricating a corrected name or source; keeping the transcribed name. name_verified=false, confidence medium.
